Deep Dive ▾own the themes
To learn how camera technique and mise-en-scène combine to construct meaning, and to practise writing analytical sentences that connect a specific technique to its effect on the audience.

1Shot Size

Pick the size that best describes this frame.

2Angle

Where is the camera, relative to the subject?

3Mise-en-scène

What has been staged deliberately in this frame?

Case File: Your Analysis

Build one sentence from technique, one from mise-en-scène, then close with your own reading.
4Camera Technique
Mise-en-scène
Your Reading
Your Paragraph
Key Terms
Worked Example
Rabbit-Proof Fence: the girls are taken
The three moves built one at a time, the way you build yours. Read what each move does before you try your own.
Camera Technique
Noyce uses an eye-level wide shot to convey the violence of the removal to the audience.
Name the shot, then its job. A wide shot holds the officer, the children and the car in one frame, so we see the whole act. Eye level puts us level with the girls, as witnesses.
Mise-en-scène
The costume reinforces the power imbalance between the officer and the girls.
One staged detail, not the whole frame. The officer's dark, buttoned uniform is the state; the girls' plain, worn dresses are the children it is taking. Placed together, they show the power gap.
Your Reading
This matters because the shot does not look away. By holding the whole act in one wide, level frame, Noyce makes the audience witness the removal instead of having it softened or explained.
This is the 'so what'. It links the shot and the costume to their effect: we are made to watch, not allowed to look away.
Technique Mise-en-scène Reading
High Ground: Moran speaks to Darrpa
The higher bar. Here the same moves, the shot, the costume, the colour, are woven into one flowing paragraph rather than listed. Aim for this once you can build the parts.

This eye level shot of Moran sitting and speaking to Darrpa visually highlights how distinctly different he is from his surroundings. From the jarring white uniform, to his badges and symbols and the unnatural green underside of his hat, the director conveys with this visual that Moran does not belong on this land. The rigidity of his uniform and badges also highlight his commitment to the law, British rules and legal systems. The contrast with the surroundings highlights how laughable this notion is, as British law makes no sense in Australian land, a completely different country with its own rules.
